abstract:
Existing long-form video generation frameworks lack automated planning,
requiring manual input for storylines, scenes, cinematography, and character
interactions, resulting in high costs and inefficiencies. To address these
challenges, we present MovieAgent, an automated movie generation via
multi-agent Chain of Thought (CoT) planning. MovieAgent offers two key
advantages: 1) We firstly explore and define the paradigm of automated
movie/long-video generation. Given a script and character bank, our MovieAgent
can generates multi-scene, multi-shot long-form videos with a coherent
narrative, while ensuring character consistency, synchronized subtitles, and
stable audio throughout the film. 2) MovieAgent introduces a hierarchical
CoT-based reasoning process to automatically structure scenes, camera settings,
and cinematography, significantly reducing human effort. By employing multiple
LLM agents to simulate the roles of a director, screenwriter, storyboard
artist, and location manager, MovieAgent streamlines the production pipeline.
Experiments demonstrate that MovieAgent achieves new state-of-the-art results
in script faithfulness, character consistency, and narrative coherence. Our
hierarchical framework takes a step forward and provides new insights into
fully automated movie generation. The code and project website are available
at: this https URL and
this https URL

keyInsights: Breaking down movie generation into hierarchical specialized tasks improves coherence
Chain of Thought reasoning enables interpretable and traceable decision-making
Multi-agent coordination can effectively replicate human filmmaking collaboration
originalProblem: Existing video generation methods struggle with long-form narrative coherence and character consistency
Traditional movie production requires extensive manual effort and coordination between specialized roles
Current approaches lack automated planning for cinematography and scene structuring
results: Achieved highest scores in motion smoothness, dynamic degree, and aesthetic quality on VBench metrics
Outperformed baselines in narrative coherence, visual appeal, and script faithfulness in expert evaluations
Successfully automated complex video generation while maintaining character and story consistency
solution: Multi-agent system with specialized agents for directing, scene planning, and shot design
Hierarchical Chain of Thought reasoning for step-by-step decision making
Customized shot and audio generation models integrated with planning framework
paper_summary.summary: Show Lab researchers at NUS introduce MovieAgent, a groundbreaking multi-agent framework that automates long-form video generation through Chain of Thought planning, achieving superior narrative coherence and visual quality while requiring minimal human intervention through innovative coordination between specialized AI agents acting as director, scene planner, and shot designer.

title: Automated Movie Generation via Multi-Agent CoT Planning

abstract:
Video-to-audio synthesis, which generates synchronized audio for visual
content, critically enhances viewer immersion and narrative coherence in film
and interactive media. However, video-to-audio dubbing for long-form content
remains an unsolved challenge due to dynamic semantic shifts, temporal
misalignment, and the absence of dedicated datasets. While existing methods
excel in short videos, they falter in long scenarios (e.g., movies) due to
fragmented synthesis and inadequate cross-scene consistency. We propose
LVAS-Agent, a novel multi-agent framework that emulates professional dubbing
workflows through collaborative role specialization. Our approach decomposes
long-video synthesis into four steps including scene segmentation, script
generation, sound design and audio synthesis. Central innovations include a
discussion-correction mechanism for scene/script refinement and a
generation-retrieval loop for temporal-semantic alignment. To enable systematic
evaluation, we introduce LVAS-Bench, the first benchmark with 207
professionally curated long videos spanning diverse scenarios. Experiments
demonstrate superior audio-visual alignment over baseline methods. Project
page: this https URL
title: Long-Video Audio Synthesis with Multi-Agent Collaboration

abstract: Instruction-based video editing promises to democratize content creation, yet its progress is severely hampered by the scarcity of large-scale, high-quality training data. We introduce Ditto, a holistic framework designed to tackle this fundamental challenge. At its heart, Ditto features a novel data generation pipeline that fuses the creative diversity of a leading image editor with an in-context video generator, overcoming the limited scope of existing models. To make this process viable, our framework resolves the prohibitive cost-quality trade-off by employing an efficient, distilled model architecture augmented by a temporal enhancer, which simultaneously reduces computational overhead and improves temporal coherence. Finally, to achieve full scalability, this entire pipeline is driven by an intelligent agent that crafts diverse instructions and rigorously filters the output, ensuring quality control at scale. Using this framework, we invested over 12,000 GPU-days to build Ditto-1M, a new dataset of one million high-fidelity video editing examples. We trained our model, Editto, on Ditto-1M with a curriculum learning strategy. The results demonstrate superior instruction-following ability and establish a new state-of-the-art in instruction-based video editing.

keyInsights: High-quality synthetic video editing data can be generated at scale by leveraging advanced image editors, depth information, and efficient video generation techniques.
Combining diverse visual contexts (edited key-frames, depth videos) with textual prompts effectively guides a video generator to produce temporally consistent and semantically accurate edits.
A Modality Curriculum Learning strategy is crucial for training models to interpret abstract text instructions effectively when the underlying data synthesis relies on more explicit visual guidance.
originalProblem: Instruction-based video editing significantly lags behind image editing due to the inherent complexities of the temporal dimension.
A profound scarcity of large-scale, high-quality, and diverse paired video-text-edit data hinders the training of robust end-to-end video editing models.
Existing video editing methods face a trade-off between computational efficiency and output quality, often resulting in expensive processes or temporal inconsistencies.
results: The Editto model achieved state-of-the-art performance, with a CLIP-T score of 25.54 (instruction following), CLIP-F of 99.03 (temporal consistency), and a VLM score of 8.10 (holistic quality), outperforming prior methods.
Human evaluations showed Editto's superior instruction-following accuracy (3.85), temporal consistency (3.76), and overall quality (3.86) compared to competitors.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of the Ditto-1M dataset's scale and the Modality Curriculum Learning in improving stylistic edit quality, content fidelity, and enabling the model to surpass the raw data generator's capabilities.
solution: Developed the multi-stage Ditto framework for generating high-quality synthetic video editing data, integrating source video filtering, automated instruction generation via VLMs, and multi-modal visual context preparation.

Employed an efficient in-context video generator, combining textual prompts, edited key-frames, and depth videos, with model quantization and knowledge distillation for scalability and cost-efficiency.

Constructed Ditto-1M, a large-scale dataset of over one million high-fidelity video editing examples, and trained the Editto model using a Modality Curriculum Learning strategy to bridge the gap from visually-conditioned data synthesis to purely language-driven inference.
paper_summary.summary: The Ditto framework introduces a scalable, cost-efficient pipeline to generate Ditto-1M, a high-quality synthetic dataset containing over one million instruction-based video editing examples. This extensive dataset enables the Editto model to achieve state-of-the-art performance across instruction adherence, temporal consistency, and visual quality for complex video modifications.

title: Scaling Instruction-Based Video Editing with a High-Quality Synthetic Dataset

abstract: Current video generation techniques excel at single-shot clips but struggle to produce narrative multi-shot videos, which require flexible shot arrangement, coherent narrative, and controllability beyond text prompts. To tackle these challenges, we propose MultiShotMaster, a framework for highly controllable multi-shot video generation. We extend a pretrained single-shot model by integrating two novel variants of RoPE. First, we introduce Multi-Shot Narrative RoPE, which applies explicit phase shift at shot transitions, enabling flexible shot arrangement while preserving the temporal narrative order. Second, we design Spatiotemporal Position-Aware RoPE to incorporate reference tokens and grounding signals, enabling spatiotemporal-grounded reference injection. In addition, to overcome data scarcity, we establish an automated data annotation pipeline to extract multi-shot videos, captions, cross-shot grounding signals and reference images. Our framework leverages the intrinsic architectural properties to support multi-shot video generation, featuring text-driven inter-shot consistency, customized subject with motion control, and background-driven customized scene. Both shot count and duration are flexibly configurable. Extensive experiments demonstrate the superior performance and outstanding controllability of our framework.

keyInsights: Modifying intrinsic architectural elements like Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) can enable complex spatiotemporal control for multi-shot video generation, circumventing the need for computationally heavy external adapters.
Explicit angular phase shifts within RoPE allow the model to inherently recognize and manage shot transitions, providing a foundation for flexible shot arrangements and narrative flow.

An automated data curation pipeline, combining computer vision and large language models, can effectively generate the complex, multi-shot, multi-reference datasets required for training sophisticated generative models.
originalProblem: Current AI video generation largely produces single-shot clips, failing to meet the demands of narrative multi-shot video production for coherent storytelling.

Existing multi-shot generation approaches (keyframe-based or end-to-end) lack flexibility in shot arrangement, struggle with consistent narrative elements, and primarily offer only text-based control.
Integrating fine-grained control signals like subject appearance, motion, and background consistency into multi-shot generation is computationally expensive with existing adapter-based methods.
results: MultiShotMaster achieved a Text Alignment score of 0.227 and inter-shot Semantic Consistency of 0.702, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in multi-shot text-to-video and reference-to-video generation.

It demonstrated highly precise shot transitions with a Transition Deviation of 1.41, validating the efficacy of the Multi-Shot Narrative RoPE, and maintained strong Subject Consistency (0.495) across shots.

The framework robustly incorporates reference subjects and backgrounds with high fidelity, achieving a 0.594 mIoU for spatiotemporal grounding accuracy, ensuring precise control over their placement and motion.
solution: MultiShotMaster modifies Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) with angular phase shifts (Multi-Shot Narrative RoPE) to explicitly control shot transitions and enable flexible shot counts and durations.
It utilizes Spatiotemporal Position-Aware RoPE to inject customized subjects with precise motion grounding and consistent backgrounds into specified video regions.
The framework employs a Multi-Shot & Multi-Reference Attention Mask and a hierarchical prompt structure to manage complex control signals and maintain narrative coherence across shots.
An automated multi-shot & multi-reference data curation pipeline and a three-stage training paradigm are used to address data scarcity and optimize model performance.
paper_summary.summary: MultiShotMaster, a framework developed by researchers from Dalian University of Technology, Kuaishou Technology's Kling Team, and The Chinese University of Hong Kong, enables controllable multi-shot video generation by modifying Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) and employing an automated data curation pipeline. The approach achieved a 0.702 semantic consistency score and a 1.41 transition deviation, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in multi-shot text-to-video and reference-to-video generation while demonstrating precise shot control and coherent narratives.

title: MultiShotMaster: A Controllable Multi-Shot Video Generation Framework

abstract: The "one-shot" technique represents a distinct and sophisticated aesthetic in filmmaking. However, its practical realization is often hindered by prohibitive costs and complex real-world constraints. Although emerging video generation models offer a virtual alternative, existing approaches typically rely on naive clip concatenation, which frequently fails to maintain visual smoothness and temporal coherence. In this paper, we introduce DreaMontage, a comprehensive framework designed for arbitrary frame-guided generation, capable of synthesizing seamless, expressive, and long-duration one-shot videos from diverse user-provided inputs. To achieve this, we address the challenge through three primary dimensions. (i) We integrate a lightweight intermediate-conditioning mechanism into the DiT architecture. By employing an Adaptive Tuning strategy that effectively leverages base training data, we unlock robust arbitrary-frame control capabilities. (ii) To enhance visual fidelity and cinematic expressiveness, we curate a high-quality dataset and implement a Visual Expression SFT stage. In addressing critical issues such as subject motion rationality and transition smoothness, we apply a Tailored DPO scheme, which significantly improves the success rate and usability of the generated content. (iii) To facilitate the production of extended sequences, we design a Segment-wise Auto-Regressive (SAR) inference strategy that operates in a memory-efficient manner. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves visually striking and seamlessly coherent one-shot effects while maintaining computational efficiency, empowering users to transform fragmented visual materials into vivid, cohesive one-shot cinematic experiences.
title: DreaMontage: Arbitrary Frame-Guided One-Shot Video Generation

abstract: Editing the video content with audio alignment forms a digital human-made art in current social media. However, the time-consuming and repetitive nature of manual video editing has long been a challenge for filmmakers and professional content creators alike. In this paper, we introduce CutClaw, an autonomous multi-agent framework designed to edit hours-long raw footage into meaningful short videos that leverages the capabilities of multiple Multimodal Language Models~(MLLMs) as an agent system. It produces videos with synchronized music, followed by instructions, and a visually appealing appearance. In detail, our approach begins by employing a hierarchical multimodal decomposition that captures both fine-grained details and global structures across visual and audio footage. Then, to ensure narrative consistency, a Playwriter Agent orchestrates the whole storytelling flow and structures the long-term narrative, anchoring visual scenes to musical shifts. Finally, to construct a short edited video, Editor and Reviewer Agents collaboratively optimize the final cut via selecting fine-grained visual content based on rigorous aesthetic and semantic criteria. We conduct detailed experiments to demonstrate that CutClaw significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in generating high-quality, rhythm-aligned videos. The code is available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Hierarchical decomposition and multi-agent collaboration effectively manage MLLM context length limitations by abstracting continuous high-dimensional data into discrete semantic units for hours-long footage.
Utilizing music structure as an invariant temporal anchor enables precise, rhythmically synchronized visual cuts and scene transitions, leading to superior audio-visual harmony.

A multi-criteria Reviewer agent, employing rejection sampling, refines candidate clips by verifying semantic identity, temporal integrity, and perceptual quality, providing iterative feedback to improve output.
originalProblem: Manually editing extensive raw footage into concise, high-quality videos is a time-consuming, repetitive process requiring complex multimodal alignment.
Existing automated video editing methods lack holistic multimodal alignment, often providing rigid structures, being audio-agnostic, or disregarding musical rhythm and narrative coherence.
Multimodal Language Models face context length limitations with hours-long footage, complicating context-grounded storytelling and fine-grained cross-modal synchronization.
results: CutClaw achieved superior quantitative performance across all metrics on a 24-hour raw footage benchmark, with scores of 77.6 for Visual Quality, 70.0 for Instruction Follow, and 86.5 for AV Harmony, surpassing baseline methods.

A user study with 25 participants confirmed CutClaw's perceived superiority, receiving 49.8% of votes for Visual Quality, 50.2% for Instruction Following, 53.0% for Audio-Visual Harmony, and 48.8% for Human-Likeness.

Ablation studies demonstrated the necessity of detailed audio context for AV Harmony, the Reviewer's feedback for visual quality, and the Editor's hierarchical structuring for narrative coherence and semantic accuracy.
solution: A multi-agent framework powered by Multimodal Language Models (MLLMs), named CutClaw, simulates a professional post-production workflow using a coarse-to-fine hierarchical approach.
A bottom-up multimodal deconstruction module discretizes video into scenes (with character-aware grounding) and music into hierarchical keypoints, forming structured semantic units.

A top-down collaborative agent system, including a Playwriter (global planner), Editor (fine-grained visual grounder), and Reviewer (multi-criteria validity gate), optimizes for visual quality, narrative flow, semantic alignment, and rhythmic alignment.
paper_summary.summary: CutClaw is an autonomous multi-agent framework that transforms hours-long raw video footage into high-quality short videos by precisely synchronizing visual cuts and pacing with background music. The system achieves superior performance, scoring 77.6 for Visual Quality, 70.0 for Instruction Follow, and 86.5 for AV Harmony, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art baselines in both quantitative metrics and user studies.

title: CutClaw: Agentic Hours-Long Video Editing via Music Synchronization

abstract: While specialized AI models excel at isolated video tasks like generation or understanding, real-world applications demand complex, iterative workflows that combine these capabilities. To bridge this gap, we introduce UniVA, an open-source, omni-capable multi-agent framework for next-generation video generalists that unifies video understanding, segmentation, editing, and generation into cohesive workflows. UniVA employs a Plan-and-Act dual-agent architecture that drives a highly automated and proactive workflow: a planner agent interprets user intentions and decomposes them into structured video-processing steps, while executor agents execute these through modular, MCP-based tool servers (for analysis, generation, editing, tracking, etc.). Through a hierarchical multi-level memory (global knowledge, task context, and user-specific preferences), UniVA sustains long-horizon reasoning, contextual continuity, and inter-agent communication, enabling interactive and self-reflective video creation with full traceability. This design enables iterative and any-conditioned video workflows (e.g., text/image/video-conditioned generation $\rightarrow$ multi-round editing $\rightarrow$ object segmentation $\rightarrow$ compositional synthesis) that were previously cumbersome to achieve with single-purpose models or monolithic video-language models. We also introduce UniVA-Bench, a benchmark suite of multi-step video tasks spanning understanding, editing, segmentation, and generation, to rigorously evaluate such agentic video systems. Both UniVA and UniVA-Bench are fully open-sourced, aiming to catalyze research on interactive, agentic, and general-purpose video intelligence for the next generation of multimodal AI systems. (this https URL)

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: A unified agentic architecture with dynamic interplay between modules is crucial for emergent video intelligence, surpassing the capabilities of isolated or monolithic models.
The explicit Plan-Act framework significantly enhances task processing, improving both the validity and quality of plans for complex video production workflows.

Hierarchical memory mechanisms are essential for persistent context, enabling better understanding of user intent, improving consistency in generated content, and preventing catastrophic planning failures.
originalProblem: Existing video AI models are highly specialized and operate in isolation, making complex, iterative video production workflows brittle, labor-intensive, and non-interactive.
Unified video-language foundation models often lack flexibility to integrate new tools or efficiently handle dynamic, multi-round video processing goals.
Prior LLM-based agent frameworks are either too general-purpose or limited to specific video tasks like understanding, failing to provide comprehensive end-to-end video production capabilities.
results: UniVA achieved 0.76 accuracy in complex long-video Q&A, outperforming leading LLMs like GPT-4o and Gemini 2.5 Pro.
The Plan-Act agent more than doubled the success rate (45.0% vs. 20.0%) and achieved over twice the quality (wPED 0.117 vs. 0.050) compared to a single-agent design.
Human evaluators consistently preferred UniVA's outputs in video generation tasks, giving it the highest scores in four out of five dimensions (e.g., Semantic Accuracy, Action/Behavior Accuracy).
solution: UniVA employs a Plan-Act dual-agent architecture, where a high-level Planner agent strategizes complex tasks, and a low-level Executor agent selects and invokes specific tools.
A hierarchical memory system (Global, Task, User) maintains context across long-horizon reasoning and multi-step workflows, improving robustness and user alignment.
The Model Context Protocol (MCP) enables a modular, plug-and-play integration of diverse state-of-the-art video and non-video tools, fostering extensibility and comprehensive functionality.
paper_summary.summary: UniVA introduces an open-source, multi-agent framework designed as a generalist for video understanding, editing, and generation, significantly improving performance on complex, multi-step video tasks. It achieved higher accuracy in long-video Q&A and superior human preference scores for video generation compared to existing models and LLM-based solutions.

title: UniVA: Universal Video Agent towards Open-Source Next-Generation Video Generalist

abstract:
In this paper, we introduce zero-shot audio-video editing, a novel task that
requires transforming original audio-visual content to align with a specified
textual prompt without additional model training. To evaluate this task, we
curate a benchmark dataset, AvED-Bench, designed explicitly for zero-shot
audio-video editing. AvED-Bench includes 110 videos, each with a 10-second
duration, spanning 11 categories from VGGSound. It offers diverse prompts and
scenarios that require precise alignment between auditory and visual elements,
enabling robust evaluation. We identify limitations in existing zero-shot audio
and video editing methods, particularly in synchronization and coherence
between modalities, which often result in inconsistent outcomes. To address
these challenges, we propose AvED, a zero-shot cross-modal delta denoising
framework that leverages audio-video interactions to achieve synchronized and
coherent edits. AvED demonstrates superior results on both AvED-Bench and the
recent OAVE dataset to validate its generalization capabilities. Results are
available at this https URL
title: Zero-Shot Audio-Visual Editing via Cross-Modal Delta Denoising

abstract:
Recent years have seen a significant increase in video content creation and
consumption. Crafting engaging content requires the careful curation of both
visual and audio elements. While visual cue curation, through techniques like
optimal viewpoint selection or post-editing, has been central to media
production, its natural counterpart, audio, has not undergone equivalent
advancements. This often results in a disconnect between visual and acoustic
saliency. To bridge this gap, we introduce a novel task: visually-guided
acoustic highlighting, which aims to transform audio to deliver appropriate
highlighting effects guided by the accompanying video, ultimately creating a
more harmonious audio-visual experience. We propose a flexible,
transformer-based multimodal framework to solve this task. To train our model,
we also introduce a new dataset -- the muddy mix dataset, leveraging the
meticulous audio and video crafting found in movies, which provides a form of
free supervision. We develop a pseudo-data generation process to simulate
poorly mixed audio, mimicking real-world scenarios through a three-step process
-- separation, adjustment, and remixing. Our approach consistently outperforms
several baselines in both quantitative and subjective evaluation. We also
systematically study the impact of different types of contextual guidance and
difficulty levels of the dataset. Our project page is here:
this https URL

keyInsights: Visual content provides valuable cues for determining appropriate audio mixing levels
Temporal context significantly improves the model's ability to create balanced audio mixes
Professional movies offer free supervision for learning audio-visual relationships
originalProblem: Most raw video recordings lack professional audio mixing, creating mismatched audio-visual experiences
Existing audio processing methods focus mainly on music or sound separation without considering visual context
results: Achieved superior performance across waveform distance and semantic alignment metrics compared to baselines
Successfully demonstrated automatic adjustment of audio levels to match visual importance
Maintained effectiveness across varied scenes and sound types while preserving natural audio qualities
Reduced need for manual audio mixing while improving overall audio-visual coherence
solution: Developed VisAH, a transformer-based model that uses visual cues to guide audio highlighting
Created THE MUDDY MIX DATASET using movie clips to train the system on professional audio-visual alignments
Implemented a dual-encoder architecture processing both spectrogram and waveform inputs
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Rochester, University of Maryland, and Meta Reality Labs introduce a transformer-based framework for automatically adjusting audio mixing in videos by learning from professionally produced movies, achieving improved audio-visual alignment while maintaining natural sound qualities across diverse scenes and sound types.

title: Learning to Highlight Audio by Watching Movies

abstract: Text-driven video generation has democratized film creation, but camera control in cinematic multi-shot scenarios remains a significant block. Implicit textual prompts lack precision, while explicit trajectory conditioning imposes prohibitive manual overhead and often triggers execution failures in current models. To overcome this bottleneck, we propose a data-centric paradigm shift, positing that aligned (Caption, Trajectory, Video) triplets form an inherent joint distribution that can connect automated plotting and precise execution. Guided by this insight, we present ShotVerse, a "Plan-then-Control" framework that decouples generation into two collaborative agents: a VLM (Vision-Language Model)-based Planner that leverages spatial priors to obtain cinematic, globally aligned trajectories from text, and a Controller that renders these trajectories into multi-shot video content via a camera adapter. Central to our approach is the construction of a data foundation: we design an automated multi-shot camera calibration pipeline aligns disjoint single-shot trajectories into a unified global coordinate system. This facilitates the curation of ShotVerse-Bench, a high-fidelity cinematic dataset with a three-track evaluation protocol that serves as the bedrock for our framework. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ShotVerse effectively bridges the gap between unreliable textual control and labor-intensive manual plotting, achieving superior cinematic aesthetics and generating multi-shot videos that are both camera-accurate and cross-shot consistent.

keyInsights: Decoupling multi-shot camera control into distinct planning (text-to-trajectory) and controlling (trajectory-to-video) phases improves stability and precision for complex cinematic tasks.

A data-centric approach, emphasizing aligned (Caption, Trajectory, Video) triplets and a novel multi-shot camera calibration pipeline, is crucial for learning global spatial coherence in cinematic video generation.
Leveraging pre-trained Vision-Language Models (VLMs) within the Planner enables effective inference of complex multi-shot narratives and generation of accurate trajectories from text.
originalProblem: Existing text-driven video generation methods lack precise and consistent cinematic camera control, often relying on imprecise implicit textual prompts or labor-intensive manual plotting.
Current models struggle to execute complex multi-shot cinematic trajectories, treating them as out-of-distribution conditions, leading to generation failures or quality degradation.
A scarcity of high-fidelity multi-shot camera trajectory datasets with global spatial coherence and unified annotations hinders the development of robust cinematic AI.
results: The ShotVerse Planner achieved an F1-Score of 0.422 and a CLaTr-CLIP score of 35.016, outperforming baseline trajectory generation methods on ShotVerse-Bench.
The Controller demonstrated superior execution fidelity with a Translation Error of 0.0163, a Rotation Error of 0.73, and the highest Coordinate Alignment Score (0.500) for cross-shot consistency.

End-to-end, ShotVerse achieved the lowest FVD (281.71), highest Aesthetic Quality (5.465), and highest Shot Transition Accuracy (0.933), surpassing commercial and open-source models in both objective metrics and human evaluations for cinematic quality.
solution: Developed ShotVerse, a "Plan-then-Control" framework that decouples camera control into a VLM-based Planner for generating globally aligned trajectories and a Controller for rendering these into multi-shot videos.
Created ShotVerse-Bench, a dataset of 20,500 clips from professional cinema with hierarchical captions and unified multi-shot trajectories, enabled by an automated camera calibration pipeline.

The Planner uses a fine-tuned VLM to process hierarchical prompts and generate variable-length trajectories, while the Controller adapts a video foundation model with a camera encoder and 4D Rotary Positional Embedding for precise execution.
paper_summary.summary: The ShotVerse framework introduces a "Plan-then-Control" approach for text-driven multi-shot video creation, enabling precise cinematic camera control. It achieved state-of-the-art performance, with an F1-Score of 0.422 for trajectory planning, a Translation Error of 0.0163 for execution fidelity, and an FVD of 281.71 for end-to-end video quality, also introducing a new high-fidelity multi-shot cinematic dataset.

title: ShotVerse: Advancing Cinematic Camera Control for Text-Driven Multi-Shot Video Creation

abstract: Recent advances in video generation have produced models capable of synthesizing stunning visual content from simple text prompts. However, these models struggle to generate long-form, coherent narratives from high-level concepts like dialogue, revealing a ``semantic gap'' between a creative idea and its cinematic execution. To bridge this gap, we introduce a novel, end-to-end agentic framework for dialogue-to-cinematic-video generation. Central to our framework is ScripterAgent, a model trained to translate coarse dialogue into a fine-grained, executable cinematic script. To enable this, we construct ScriptBench, a new large-scale benchmark with rich multimodal context, annotated via an expert-guided pipeline. The generated script then guides DirectorAgent, which orchestrates state-of-the-art video models using a cross-scene continuous generation strategy to ensure long-horizon coherence. Our comprehensive evaluation, featuring an AI-powered CriticAgent and a new Visual-Script Alignment (VSA) metric, shows our framework significantly improves script faithfulness and temporal fidelity across all tested video models. Furthermore, our analysis uncovers a crucial trade-off in current SOTA models between visual spectacle and strict script adherence, providing valuable insights for the future of automated filmmaking.

keyInsights: A script-centric, planning-based approach effectively bridges the "semantic gap" in automated filmmaking, enabling AI to produce long-form narratives aligned with directorial intent.

The combination of supervised fine-tuning with reinforcement learning and a hybrid reward function significantly enhances both the technical correctness and subjective artistic quality of AI-generated cinematic scripts.
Employing a frame-anchored continuity mechanism across segmented scenes is crucial for mitigating identity drift and maintaining visual and narrative coherence over extended video durations.
There exists a fundamental trade-off in state-of-the-art video models between achieving high visual spectacle and strictly adhering to script details and narrative consistency.
originalProblem: Existing video generation models struggle with a "semantic gap," failing to translate high-level narrative concepts from dialogue into coherent, long-form cinematic realizations.
Current methods lack the ability to maintain narrative, character, and visual consistency over extended video durations due to their restricted temporal capacity.
Automated filmmaking requires resolving fine-grained contextual ambiguities from sparse dialogue and integrating specialized domain knowledge for cinematic planning.
results: The ScripterAgent model achieved superior script quality, outperforming baselines with up to +0.4 points in AI-rated metrics and higher human scores in Visual Imagery (4.3 vs. 3.8) and Dramatic Tension (4.1 vs. 3.7).

Conditioning state-of-the-art video models with ScripterAgent-generated scripts resulted in a universal uplift in video quality, increasing mean AI ratings from 4.2 to 4.5 and human ratings from 3.7 to 4.2.

Enhanced Script Faithfulness (e.g., Wan2.6 improved from 3.2 to 4.0, Sora2-Pro from 3.6 to 4.2), Character Consistency, and Narrative Coherence were consistently observed, quantitatively validated by the novel Visual-Script Alignment (VSA) metric.
solution: A novel end-to-end agentic framework composed of three specialized AI agents: ScripterAgent (dialogue-to-script generation), DirectorAgent (script-to-video execution), and CriticAgent (evaluation).

ScripterAgent is trained on a new ScriptBench dataset using a two-stage paradigm (Supervised Fine-Tuning + Reinforcement Learning with a hybrid reward function) to generate fine-grained cinematic scripts.

DirectorAgent implements a Cross-Scene Continuous Generation Strategy with intelligent shot-based segmentation and a frame-anchored continuity mechanism to overcome fixed-duration limitations of video models and ensure long-horizon coherence.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tencent Hunyuan Multimodal Department and Xidian University developed an end-to-end agentic framework for generating long-horizon cinematic videos directly from dialogue. This system utilizes specialized AI agents to produce detailed, professional-quality scripts and then orchestrates state-of-the-art video models to achieve enhanced video coherence, script faithfulness, and dynamic scene generation.

title: The Script is All You Need: An Agentic Framework for Long-Horizon Dialogue-to-Cinematic Video Generation

abstract: While end-to-end video-to-audio generation has greatly improved, producing high-fidelity audio that authentically captures the nuances of visual content remains challenging. Like professionals in the creative industries, this generation requires sophisticated reasoning about items such as visual dynamics, acoustic environments, and temporal relationships. We present ThinkSound, a novel framework that leverages Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to enable stepwise, interactive audio generation and editing for videos. Our approach decomposes the process into three complementary stages: foundational foley generation that creates semantically coherent soundscapes, interactive object-centric refinement through precise user interactions, and targeted editing guided by natural language instructions. At each stage, a multimodal large language model generates contextually aligned CoT reasoning that guides a unified audio foundation model. Furthermore, we introduce AudioCoT, a comprehensive dataset with structured reasoning annotations that establishes connections between visual content, textual descriptions, and sound synthesis. Experiments demonstrate that ThinkSound achieves state-of-the-art performance in video-to-audio generation across both audio metrics and CoT metrics, and excels in the out-of-distribution Movie Gen Audio benchmark. The project page is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning from MLLMs significantly enhances video-to-audio generation and editing by providing explicit, actionable steps that capture sound events, temporal relationships, and acoustic characteristics.

A unified multimodal audio foundation model, leveraging flow matching and a hybrid transformer architecture, can effectively integrate diverse inputs and respond to CoT instructions for high-quality audio synthesis across multiple interactive stages.

Structured, audio-specific reasoning annotations, as provided by the AudioCoT dataset, are crucial for training MLLMs to achieve advanced audio-centric understanding and decompose complex audio design tasks.
originalProblem: Existing video-to-audio (V2A) generation models often produce generic sounds, lack precise temporal synchronization with visual content, and offer limited user control.
Previous MLLM-based V2A efforts either lose visual details or fragment the process into separate tasks, failing to fully leverage deep multimodal understanding for comprehensive audio design.
There was a lack of large-scale datasets with explicit reasoning annotations to train MLLMs for audio-specific Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in V2A tasks.
results: ThinkSound outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on the VGGSound test set, achieving lower KL Divergence (e.g., KL$_{PaSST}$: 1.52 vs. 1.65 for MMAudio) and higher subjective quality (MOS-Q: 4.02 vs. 3.84) and alignment (MOS-A: 4.18 vs. 3.97).
Ablation studies confirm the critical role of CoT reasoning, with its removal causing a notable degradation in audio quality and alignment (e.g., CLAP$_{CoT}$ drops from 0.46 to 0.41).

The framework demonstrates robust generalization on out-of-distribution datasets (MovieGen Audio Bench) and achieves superior performance in object-focused audio generation and text-guided editing tasks, outperforming AudioLDM-2 and Edit Friendly DDPM.
solution: ThinkSound employs a three-stage interactive workflow: foundational foley generation, object-centric refinement, and instruction-based editing, all guided by MLLM-generated Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning.
A unified audio foundation model, trained with conditional flow matching, synthesizes high-fidelity audio, conditioned on multimodal inputs (video, CoT, text captions, existing audio context).
The novel AudioCoT dataset, featuring structured CoT reasoning annotations, was constructed to fine-tune MLLMs for audio-specific understanding and structured decomposition of audio design tasks.
paper_summary.summary: ThinkSound introduces a three-stage interactive framework for video-to-audio generation and editing, employing Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning from Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to guide a unified audio foundation model. This approach generates higher fidelity audio that is more semantically aligned and temporally precise than prior methods, while also enabling fine-grained user control.

title: ThinkSound: Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in Multimodal Large Language Models for Audio Generation and Editing

abstract: Diffusion models have recently advanced video editing, yet controllable editing remains challenging due to the need for precise manipulation of diverse object properties. Current methods require different control signal for diverse editing tasks, which complicates model design and demands significant training resources. To address this, we propose O-DisCo-Edit, a unified framework that incorporates a novel object distortion control (O-DisCo). This signal, based on random and adaptive noise, flexibly encapsulates a wide range of editing cues within a single representation. Paired with a "copy-form" preservation module for preserving non-edited regions, O-DisCo-Edit enables efficient, high-fidelity editing through an effective training paradigm. Extensive experiments and comprehensive human evaluations consistently demonstrate that O-DisCo-Edit surpasses both specialized and multitask state-of-the-art methods across various video editing tasks. this https URL

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Unifying diverse editing cues into a single, adaptable noise-based "Object Distortion Control" (O-DisCo) signal simplifies the complexity of multi-task video editing without requiring task-specific inputs.

Randomly distorting the reference video's edited regions during training (R-O-DisCo) forces the model to learn robust video generation primarily from the first frame, reducing reliance on precise reference details.
Direct integration of non-edited region latents (CFP) and identity-specific tokens/resampling (IDP) effectively preserves video consistency and object identity across complex edits.
originalProblem: Existing video editing models are often task-specific, leading to limited versatility and requiring different complex control signals for various modifications.
Multi-task video editing frameworks typically demand complex training pipelines, large specialized datasets, and extensive computational resources.
Current methods struggle with flexible control during inference, often processing only one condition at a time, limiting the effective use of complementary cues.
results: Achieved the best or second-best quantitative performance across eight diverse video editing tasks, including object removal, outpainting, and internal motion transfer.
Demonstrated superior perceptual quality with consistently higher Mean Opinion Scores (MOS) for editing completeness and video quality compared to state-of-the-art specialized and multi-task methods.

Ablation studies confirmed that the Copy-Form Preservation (CFP) and the combined Adaptive Object Distortion Control (A-O-DisCo) and Identity Preservation (IDP) modules individually and synergistically enhance video quality and consistency.
solution: Introduces Object Distortion Control (O-DisCo), a unified noise-based signal, which is randomly distorted during training (R-O-DisCo) and adaptively controlled during inference (A-O-DisCo).
Implements a "Copy-Form" Preservation (CFP) module to flawlessly maintain non-edited video regions by integrating their latent representations directly into the main network branch.
Develops an "Identity Preservation" (IDP) module using ID tokens and ID-Resample to ensure object appearance consistency in edited regions during complex motions and transformations.
paper_summary.summary: The O-DisCo-Edit framework introduces a unified object distortion control signal to streamline multi-task video editing, significantly enhancing control and realism while reducing training resource demands. It achieves superior quantitative and perceptual performance compared to state-of-the-art methods across eight diverse editing tasks, including object removal, outpainting, and motion transfer.

title: O-DisCo-Edit: Object Distortion Control for Unified Realistic Video Editing

abstract:
Video editing according to instructions is a highly challenging task due to
the difficulty in collecting large-scale, high-quality edited video pair data.
This scarcity not only limits the availability of training data but also
hinders the systematic exploration of model architectures and training
strategies. While prior work has improved specific aspects of video editing
(e.g., synthesizing a video dataset using image editing techniques or
decomposed video editing training), a holistic framework addressing the above
challenges remains underexplored. In this study, we introduce InstructVEdit, a
full-cycle instructional video editing approach that: (1) establishes a
reliable dataset curation workflow to initialize training, (2) incorporates two
model architectural improvements to enhance edit quality while preserving
temporal consistency, and (3) proposes an iterative refinement strategy
leveraging real-world data to enhance generalization and minimize train-test
discrepancies. Extensive experiments show that InstructVEdit achieves
state-of-the-art performance in instruction-based video editing, demonstrating
robust adaptability to diverse real-world scenarios. Project page:
this https URL

keyInsights: The quality and coherence of synthetic training data are more critical than sheer volume, as demonstrated by InstructVEdit's smaller, high-quality dataset outperforming larger, lower-quality alternatives.
Effectively balancing edit fidelity and temporal consistency in video editing requires flexible integration of motion priors (SMA) and intelligent propagation of optimal edits across frames (EPM).
Iterative refinement using real-world videos is a practical and effective method for improving model generalization and adaptability to diverse, uncurated scenarios.
originalProblem: Scarcity of large-scale, high-quality, naturally supervised video editing data pairs for model training.
Challenges in simultaneously achieving high-fidelity edits and maintaining visual temporal consistency across frames.
A significant generalization gap between models trained on synthetic data and their performance on diverse, uncurated real-world videos.
results: InstructVEdit consistently achieves the highest scores across most key metrics (ViCLIP_out, ViCLIP_dir, PickScore, CLIPText) on TGVE and TGVE+ benchmarks, outperforming state-of-the-art training-based and training-free baselines.

Ablation studies show that the novel data curation workflow significantly improves editing quality, and the combined use of SMA and EPM yields the strongest performance in balancing edit fidelity and temporal consistency.

The multi-round iterative refinement strategy progressively enhances the model's generalization capabilities, with later stages of real-world-rooted synthetic data even surpassing the utility of the initial purely synthetic dataset.
solution: A novel dataset curation workflow that leverages image editing and a First-Frame-Guided Video-to-Video (FFG-V2V) model to generate high-quality, temporally consistent synthetic video editing pairs.
A new model architecture incorporating a Soft Motion Adapter (SMA) to dynamically integrate motion priors and an Editing-guided Propagation Module (EPM) to enhance spatiotemporal coherence.
A multi-round iterative refinement strategy that progressively trains the model using real-world video data to bridge the synthetic-real domain gap and improve generalization.
paper_summary.summary: InstructVEdit introduces a comprehensive, full-cycle pipeline for instructional video editing, integrating a novel dataset curation workflow, architectural model enhancements, and an iterative refinement strategy. This system achieves state-of-the-art performance on established TGVE and TGVE+ benchmarks, demonstrating improved edit fidelity and temporal consistency when modifying videos via natural language instructions.

title: InstructVEdit: A Holistic Approach for Instructional Video Editing

abstract:
Audio is inherently temporal and closely synchronized with the visual world,
making it a naturally aligned and expressive control signal for controllable
video generation (e.g., movies). Beyond control, directly translating audio
into video is essential for understanding and visualizing rich audio narratives
(e.g., Podcasts or historical recordings). However, existing approaches fall
short in generating high-quality videos with precise audio-visual
synchronization, especially across diverse and complex audio types. In this
work, we introduce MTV, a versatile framework for audio-sync video generation.
MTV explicitly separates audios into speech, effects, and music tracks,
enabling disentangled control over lip motion, event timing, and visual mood,
respectively -- resulting in fine-grained and semantically aligned video
generation. To support the framework, we additionally present DEMIX, a dataset
comprising high-quality cinematic videos and demixed audio tracks. DEMIX is
structured into five overlapped subsets, enabling scalable multi-stage training
for diverse generation scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MTV
achieves state-of-the-art performance across six standard metrics spanning
video quality, text-video consistency, and audio-video alignment. Project page:
this https URL

keyInsights: Audio demixing enables more precise audio-visual mapping by separating different types of audio signals that control distinct visual elements
Separate processing streams for interval-based features (speech/effects) versus holistic features (music) significantly improves both temporal alignment and overall visual quality
Multi-stage training from basic face movements to complex environmental scenarios allows progressive learning of increasingly sophisticated audio-visual relationships
originalProblem: Existing audio-sync video generation methods suffer from under-specified audio-visual mapping, struggling to establish precise connections between diverse audio inputs and corresponding visual features
Current approaches achieve only broad scene-level consistency but fail at accurate temporal alignment between individual audio events and their specific visual counterparts
results: Achieves 26.22% audio-video consistency compared to 6.71% for TempoTokens and superior lip synchronization with Sync-C 3.17 versus 1.45 for competitors

Demonstrates versatile applications including character creation, keyframe guidance, long video generation, and scene transitions with fine-grained controllability over appearance, lip motion, event timing, and visual mood
Outperforms state-of-the-art methods across all six evaluation metrics including FVD (626.06 vs 795.88+ for baselines) and temporal consistency (95.40% vs ~93% for competitors)
solution: Explicit audio demixing into speech, effects, and music tracks to enable disentangled control over different visual aspects
Multi-Stream Temporal ControlNet with separate interval stream (for lip motion and event timing) and holistic stream (for visual mood) processing mechanisms
DEMIX dataset with 392K cinematic video clips featuring demixed audio tracks and multi-stage training strategy across five overlapping subsets
paper_summary.summary: Beijing Academy of Artificial Intelligence and Peking University researchers develop MTV (Multi-stream Temporal control for audio-sync Video generation), a framework that achieves cinematic-quality video generation with precise audio-visual synchronization by explicitly separating audio into speech, effects, and music tracks for disentangled control over lip motion, event timing, and visual mood respectively, achieving state-of-the-art performance with 26.22% audio-video consistency (versus 6.71% for TempoTokens) and superior lip synchronization (Sync-C 3.17 vs 1.45) through a Multi-Stream Temporal ControlNet that processes demixed audio via separate interval and holistic streams integrated into CogVideoX's diffusion transformer, supported by the new DEMIX dataset containing 392K cinematic video clips with carefully demixed audio tracks and a multi-stage training strategy that progressively learns from basic face movements to complex environmental ambiance across five overlapping subsets.

title: Audio-Sync Video Generation with Multi-Stream Temporal Control

abstract:
Video editing increasingly demands the ability to incorporate specific
real-world instances into existing footage, yet current approaches
fundamentally fail to capture the unique visual characteristics of particular
subjects and ensure natural instance/scene interactions. We formalize this
overlooked yet critical editing paradigm as "Get-In-Video Editing", where users
provide reference images to precisely specify visual elements they wish to
incorporate into videos. Addressing this task's dual challenges, severe
training data scarcity and technical challenges in maintaining spatiotemporal
coherence, we introduce three key contributions. First, we develop GetIn-1M
dataset created through our automated Recognize-Track-Erase pipeline, which
sequentially performs video captioning, salient instance identification, object
detection, temporal tracking, and instance removal to generate high-quality
video editing pairs with comprehensive annotations (reference image, tracking
mask, instance prompt). Second, we present GetInVideo, a novel end-to-end
framework that leverages a diffusion transformer architecture with 3D full
attention to process reference images, condition videos, and masks
simultaneously, maintaining temporal coherence, preserving visual identity, and
ensuring natural scene interactions when integrating reference objects into
videos. Finally, we establish GetInBench, the first comprehensive benchmark for
Get-In-Video Editing scenario, demonstrating our approach's superior
performance through extensive evaluations. Our work enables accessible,
high-quality incorporation of specific real-world subjects into videos,
significantly advancing personalized video editing capabilities.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Reference images provide more precise control than text descriptions for specific instance insertion
Combining instance tracking with temporal consistency preservation improves insertion quality
Pre-trained knowledge can be effectively leveraged through careful architectural design
originalProblem: Existing video editing methods struggle to incorporate specific real-world instances while maintaining visual quality
Text-based approaches cannot capture subtle visual details of specific objects
Lack of large-scale datasets for training instance-specific video editing models
results: Superior performance across multiple metrics (FID, FVD, CLIP-I, DINO-I, CLIP-T) compared to existing approaches
Successful preservation of visual details while maintaining temporal coherence
Effective handling of both single-object and multi-object reference scenarios
Creation of GetInBench benchmark for standardized evaluation of instance-specific video editing
solution: Novel diffusion transformer architecture that processes reference images, condition videos, and masks simultaneously
Automated "Recognize-Track-Erase" pipeline to create GetIn-1M dataset of 1M video editing pairs
Integration of pre-trained T2V models with latent space concatenation for multimodal processing
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from WeChat and multiple Chinese universities introduce GetInVideo, a framework that enables precise insertion of specific real-world objects into videos using reference images, while maintaining temporal consistency and visual fidelity through an innovative diffusion transformer architecture and a million-scale automated training dataset.

title: Get In Video: Add Anything You Want to the Video

abstract: The advent of AI-Generated Content (AIGC) has spurred research into automated video generation to streamline conventional processes. However, automating storytelling video production, particularly for customized narratives, remains challenging due to the complexity of maintaining subject consistency across shots. While existing approaches like Mora and AesopAgent integrate multiple agents for Story-to-Video (S2V) generation, they fall short in preserving protagonist consistency and supporting Customized Storytelling Video Generation (CSVG). To address these limitations, we propose StoryAgent, a multi-agent framework designed for CSVG. StoryAgent decomposes CSVG into distinct subtasks assigned to specialized agents, mirroring the professional production process. Notably, our framework includes agents for story design, storyboard generation, video creation, agent coordination, and result evaluation. Leveraging the strengths of different models, StoryAgent enhances control over the generation process, significantly improving character consistency. Specifically, we introduce a customized Image-to-Video (I2V) method, LoRA-BE, to enhance intra-shot temporal consistency, while a novel storyboard generation pipeline is proposed to maintain subject consistency across shots. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in synthesizing highly consistent storytelling videos, outperforming state-of-the-art methods. Our contributions include the introduction of StoryAgent, a versatile framework for video generation tasks, and novel techniques for preserving protagonist consistency.

organization_info.name: Mohamed bin Zayed University of Artificial Intelligence
King Abdullah University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Decomposing complex video generation into distinct subtasks managed by specialized agents, mirroring a production pipeline, enhances control and quality, particularly for subject consistency.
Explicitly removing and redrawing subjects on storyboards with a fine-tuned diffusion model (StoryAnyDoor) effectively preserves customized subject identity across different narrative scenes.

Combining LoRA with block-wise token embeddings and a localization loss significantly improves the ability of I2V models to maintain high-fidelity customized subjects within individual video clips, even for 'out-domain' characters.
originalProblem: Existing AI-driven video generation models struggle to maintain consistent subject appearance throughout multi-shot narratives, especially for user-defined or 'customized' protagonists.
Current multi-agent Story-to-Video (S2V) systems lack native support for customized subjects and exhibit inconsistent protagonist appearances across different shots.

Specialized customized Text-to-Video (T2V) methods often fail to accurately preserve the fidelity of reference subjects and ensure inter-shot and intra-shot consistency across varied scenes and actions.
results: StoryAgent achieved significantly lower FVD scores (e.g., 2070.56 on PororoSV vs. 2634.01 for SVD) and higher SSIM/PSNR, indicating superior perceptual quality and structural similarity to ground truth videos.

User studies consistently rated StoryAgent highest across all metrics, particularly for Inter-shot Subject Consistency (IRC: 4.6/5) and Intra-shot Subject Consistency (IAC: 4.8/5) on open-domain datasets.

The system achieved the highest CLIP-score (0.2053) among compared methods, demonstrating better text-video alignment, and ablation studies confirmed LoRA-BE's critical role in preserving intra-shot subject consistency.
solution: StoryAgent employs a multi-agent collaborative framework with five specialized agents (Agent Manager, Story Designer, Storyboard Generator, Video Creator, Observer) that mimic a professional video production pipeline.

A novel storyboard generator pipeline utilizes a 'removal and redrawing' strategy: segmenting initial subjects from generated images and inserting the customized subject via a fine-tuned StoryAnyDoor model to ensure inter-shot consistency.

The video creator introduces LoRA-BE (Low-Rank Adaptation with Block-wise Embeddings) for customized Image-to-Video (I2V) generation, combining LoRA parameters with 16 distinct block-wise token embeddings and a localization loss to maintain intra-shot subject consistency.
paper_summary.summary: StoryAgent is a multi-agent framework that generates customized storytelling videos, enabling high subject consistency across multiple shots and within individual video clips for user-defined characters. The system achieves superior perceptual quality, subject fidelity, and text-video alignment, significantly outperforming prior methods on both public and open-domain datasets.

title: StoryAgent: Customized Storytelling Video Generation via Multi-Agent Collaboration

abstract: The rapid growth of online video content, especially on short video platforms, has created a growing demand for efficient video editing techniques that can condense long-form videos into concise and engaging clips. Existing automatic editing methods predominantly rely on textual cues from ASR transcripts and end-to-end segment selection, often neglecting the rich visual context and leading to incoherent outputs. In this paper, we propose a human-inspired automatic video editing framework (HIVE) that leverages multimodal narrative understanding to address these limitations. Our approach incorporates character extraction, dialogue analysis, and narrative summarization through multimodal large language models, enabling a holistic understanding of the video content. To further enhance coherence, we apply scene-level segmentation and decompose the editing process into three subtasks: highlight detection, opening/ending selection, and pruning of irrelevant content. To facilitate research in this area, we introduce DramaAD, a novel benchmark dataset comprising over 800 short drama episodes and 500 professionally edited advertisement clips. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework consistently outperforms existing baselines across both general and advertisement-oriented editing tasks, significantly narrowing the quality gap between automatic and human-edited videos.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

keyInsights: Comprehensive multimodal narrative understanding, combining visual and textual data, is crucial for generating coherent and engaging video edits that a single modality cannot achieve.
Decomposing the complex video editing process into sequential, human-like sub-tasks allows LLMs to perform more nuanced reasoning, leading to higher quality and more interpretable results.
Strategic selection of highlights and precise definition of opening/ending boundaries are critical for maximizing viewer engagement and optimizing clips for specific purposes like advertising.
originalProblem: Existing automatic video editing methods often rely solely on ASR, neglecting crucial visual cues and leading to incomplete narrative understanding and incoherent outputs.
Current end-to-end editing models lack systematic, human-like strategies, resulting in abrupt transitions and a failure to emulate the nuanced decision-making of professional editors.
The surge in online video content, especially for short video platforms, creates a massive demand for efficient video editing that manual processes cannot meet.
results: The HIVE framework consistently outperformed both ASR-based and narration-based end-to-end baselines on the DramaAD dataset, achieving a Viewing Experience Index (VEI) of 4.01 compared to 0.54 and 1.14, respectively.
HIVE demonstrated superior performance in advertisement-specific metrics, with a Hook Rate of 0.71 and Suspense Rate of 0.73, significantly higher than the baselines.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical importance of highlight localization, boundary selection, and content pruning, showing significant drops in engagement and advertising effectiveness when these components were removed.
solution: Developed HIVE, a two-stage framework that first performs multimodal narrative understanding using ASR, OCR, character recognition, speaker diarization, and MLLMs for deep content analysis.
Implemented a human-inspired editing framework that decomposes the task into LLM-driven sequential sub-tasks: highlight detection, opening and ending selection, and content pruning.
Introduced DramaAD, a novel benchmark dataset comprising short drama videos and their professionally edited advertisement clips, complete with multimodal narration and timestamps.
paper_summary.summary: The HIVE framework from USTC and ByteDance China converts long-form videos into concise, engaging short clips by employing a human-inspired editing strategy informed by comprehensive multimodal narrative understanding. It outperforms prior end-to-end automatic editing methods, demonstrating higher viewing experience and enhanced advertising metrics on the newly introduced DramaAD dataset.

title: From Long Videos to Engaging Clips: A Human-Inspired Video Editing Framework with Multimodal Narrative Understanding

abstract:
Despite the recent advancement in video stylization, most existing methods
struggle to render any video with complex transitions, based on an open style
description of user query. To fill this gap, we introduce a generic multi-agent
system for video stylization, V-Stylist, by a novel collaboration and
reflection paradigm of multi-modal large language models. Specifically, our
V-Stylist is a systematical workflow with three key roles: (1) Video Parser
decomposes the input video into a number of shots and generates their text
prompts of key shot content. Via a concise video-to-shot prompting paradigm, it
allows our V-Stylist to effectively handle videos with complex transitions. (2)
Style Parser identifies the style in the user query and progressively search
the matched style model from a style tree. Via a robust tree-of-thought
searching paradigm, it allows our V-Stylist to precisely specify vague style
preference in the open user query. (3) Style Artist leverages the matched model
to render all the video shots into the required style. Via a novel multi-round
self-reflection paradigm, it allows our V-Stylist to adaptively adjust detail
control, according to the style requirement. With such a distinct design of
mimicking human professionals, our V-Stylist achieves a major breakthrough over
the primary challenges for effective and automatic video stylization.
Moreover,we further construct a new benchmark Text-driven Video Stylization
Benchmark (TVSBench), which fills the gap to assess stylization of complex
videos on open user queries. Extensive experiments show that, V-Stylist
achieves the state-of-the-art, e.g.,V-Stylist surpasses FRESCO and ControlVideo
by 6.05% and 4.51% respectively in overall average metrics, marking a
significant advance in video stylization.

keyInsights: Mimicking human professional workflows with collaborative and reflective AI agents can effectively tackle complex generative tasks like video stylization.
Decomposing complex video stylization into distinct stages (parsing, style identification, adaptive rendering) through specialized MLLM agents enhances robustness and versatility.
Adaptive control and self-reflection mechanisms are crucial for fine-tuning visual details and ensuring style fidelity while preserving original content in diverse stylization scenarios.
originalProblem: Existing video stylization methods struggle with complex video transitions and long videos, often failing to maintain coherence across scene changes.
User queries for artistic styles are often vague or open-ended, leading to inaccurate stylization results from current models that lack nuanced interpretation.
Fixed detail control mechanisms in current methods result in visual distortions or content shifts, as different styles require varying levels of structural adherence.
results: V-Stylist outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on the new TVSBench benchmark by 6.05% over FRESCO and 4.51% over ControlVideo in overall average metrics.
It consistently achieves superior performance across metrics for condition alignment, temporal consistency, and video quality, demonstrating high visual quality with minimal artifacts.

Ablation studies confirm that each component (Video Parser, Style Parser, Style Artist) contributes significantly to the overall performance, with the Style Reflection mechanism adaptively adjusting ControlNet weights for improved style fidelity.
solution: V-Stylist introduces a multi-agent system with a Video Parser to decompose input videos into shots and generate content-specific text prompts using MLLMs.
A Style Parser employs a tree-of-thought searching paradigm with an MLLM to interpret ambiguous user style queries and select the most appropriate pre-trained style model from a dynamic 'style tree'.
A Style Artist renders stylized video shots using an MLLM-driven multi-round self-reflection mechanism, which adaptively adjusts ControlNet weights for precise detail control and style adherence.
paper_summary.summary: V-Stylist, developed by researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and Chinese Academy of Sciences, introduces a multi-agent system powered by Multi-modal Large Language Models to precisely stylize any video into any user-specified artistic style. The system significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods on the newly introduced TVSBench benchmark, demonstrating superior performance in style alignment, temporal consistency, and overall video quality.

title: V-Stylist: Video Stylization via Collaboration and Reflection of MLLM  Agents

abstract: Music-to-Video (M2V) generation for full-length songs faces significant challenges. Existing methods produce short, disjointed clips, failing to align visuals with musical structure, beats, or lyrics, and lack temporal consistency. We propose AutoMV, a multi-agent system that generates full music videos (MVs) directly from a song. AutoMV first applies music processing tools to extract musical attributes, such as structure, vocal tracks, and time-aligned lyrics, and constructs these features as contextual inputs for following agents. The screenwriter Agent and director Agent then use this information to design short script, define character profiles in a shared external bank, and specify camera instructions. Subsequently, these agents call the image generator for keyframes and different video generators for "story" or "singer" scenes. A Verifier Agent evaluates their output, enabling multi-agent collaboration to produce a coherent longform MV. To evaluate M2V generation, we further propose a benchmark with four high-level categories (Music Content, Technical, Post-production, Art) and twelve ine-grained criteria. This benchmark was applied to compare commercial products, AutoMV, and human-directed MVs with expert human raters: AutoMV outperforms current baselines significantly across all four categories, narrowing the gap to professional MVs. Finally, we investigate using large multimodal models as automatic MV judges; while promising, they still lag behind human expert, highlighting room for future work.

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Multi-agent coordination driven by comprehensive music-aware reasoning can successfully tackle complex, long-form cross-modal generation challenges.
Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate capability as orchestrators for high-level planning and as effective iterative verifiers for quality control in generative pipelines.
Maintaining character identity across diverse scenes requires dedicated character banks, while lyric-driven narrative planning enhances music-visual alignment and storytelling.
originalProblem: Existing video generation methods struggle to produce long-term temporally coherent and narrative-driven content suitable for full-length music.
Generated visuals often lack meaningful synchronization with crucial musical elements like beats, structure, lyrics, and overall aesthetic.
Professional music video production is expensive and complex, limiting access for independent artists and low-budget creators.
A comprehensive framework to objectively evaluate music-video alignment, musicality, and creativity in generated content was missing.
results: AutoMV significantly outperformed commercial baselines in human expert ratings across all evaluation categories, including technical quality, post-production, music content, and artistic merit.
Achieved a 24.4% ImageBind score, indicating a stronger visual-music correspondence compared to commercial baselines which scored 19.9% and 18.5%.
Reduced the estimated cost for generating a music video to $10-20 per song, providing a cost-effective solution for independent creators.
A novel 12-criterion LLM-based evaluation framework demonstrated strong alignment with human expert judgments, providing a scalable method for assessing artistic M2V outputs.
solution: Deployed a training-free, multi-agent system that orchestrates existing foundation models and tools for music video generation.
Incorporated comprehensive music informatics preprocessing to extract musical attributes, structure, and lyrics, using them to drive the visual narrative.
Utilized LLM-based Screenwriter and Director agents for shot segmentation, character design, and detailed video script generation, with an iterative Gemini Verifier agent for quality control.
paper_summary.summary: AutoMV is a training-free, multi-agent system that autonomously creates coherent, full-length music videos directly from a song by integrating music informatics with LLM-based planning and verification. The system from Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications and collaborators significantly outperforms commercial baselines in expert evaluations for technical quality, content alignment, and artistic metrics, while offering an accessible content creation solution.

title: AutoMV: An Automatic Multi-Agent System for Music Video Generation

abstract: Multi-object video motion transfer poses significant challenges for Diffusion Transformer (DiT) architectures due to inherent motion entanglement and lack of object-level control. We present MultiMotion, a novel unified framework that overcomes these limitations. Our core innovation is Maskaware Attention Motion Flow (AMF), which utilizes SAM2 masks to explicitly disentangle and control motion features for multiple objects within the DiT pipeline. Furthermore, we introduce RectPC, a high-order predictor-corrector solver for efficient and accurate sampling, particularly beneficial for multi-entity generation. To facilitate rigorous evaluation, we construct the first benchmark dataset specifically for DiT-based multi-object motion transfer. MultiMotion demonstrably achieves precise, semantically aligned, and temporally coherent motion transfer for multiple distinct objects, maintaining DiT's high quality and scalability. The code is in the supp.
title: MultiMotion: Multi Subject Video Motion Transfer via Video Diffusion Transformer

abstract:
Music videos, as a prevalent form of multimedia entertainment, deliver
engaging audio-visual experiences to audiences and have gained immense
popularity among singers and fans. Creators can express their interpretations
of music naturally through visual elements. However, the creation process of
music video demands proficiency in script design, video shooting, and
music-video synchronization, posing significant challenges for
non-professionals. Previous work has designed automated music video generation
frameworks. However, they suffer from complexity in input and poor output
quality. In response, we present MV-Crafter, a system capable of producing
high-quality music videos with synchronized music-video rhythm and style. Our
approach involves three technical modules that simulate the human creation
process: the script generation module, video generation module, and music-video
synchronization module. MV-Crafter leverages a large language model to generate
scripts considering the musical semantics. To address the challenge of
synchronizing short video clips with music of varying lengths, we propose a
dynamic beat matching algorithm and visual envelope-induced warping method to
ensure precise, monotonic music-video synchronization. Besides, we design a
user-friendly interface to simplify the creation process with intuitive editing
features. Extensive experiments have demonstrated that MV-Crafter provides an
effective solution for improving the quality of generated music videos.

keyInsights: Integrating music captions and style keywords into LLM prompts significantly enhances the narrative script's alignment with musical semantics and maintains visual consistency across scenes.
A two-stage generation process, from text-to-image to image-to-video, effectively balances visual quality, generation efficiency, and user control over scene content.

Dynamic beat matching combined with a visual envelope-induced warping algorithm enables precise, monotonic synchronization between diverse video and music clips, avoiding common issues like repetitive frames.
originalProblem: Traditional music video production demands specialized skills in scriptwriting, cinematography, and intricate music-video synchronization, creating high barriers for non-professionals.

Existing automated music video generation methods often lack visual quality, narrative coherence, content diversity, or precise synchronization between complex musical rhythms and varying video clip lengths.
Current synchronization approaches frequently lead to awkward transitions, repetitive frames, or an inability to capture nuanced "cut-to-the-beat" aesthetics.
results: MV-Crafter achieved a superior Beat Alignment Score (BAS) of 0.777, significantly outperforming baseline methods in music-video synchronization.

The system demonstrated higher semantic alignment with input themes (CLIPSIM 18.268) and received significantly higher user ratings for overall quality, theme correspondence, and narrative coherence compared to baselines.
Ablation studies confirmed the critical role of integrating music captions and style keywords during LLM script generation for enhancing music correspondence and visual consistency.
solution: MV-Crafter implements a modular, three-step pipeline: script generation (using GPT-4 for narrative and musical semantics), video generation (using Stable Diffusion XL and Stable Video Diffusion), and dynamic music-video synchronization.
